Scientists just discovered a monster black hole collision. It's the biggest that we've ever seen. Look at this chimpanzee. It's using a stick to eat termites. And this one is using a leaf to scoop water. We're about to see a super moon. This only happens a few times a year, because to get a super moon, two things need to happen at the same time. First, you need a full moon. These children are test subjects, and this experiment could save your life. So in 1941, there was this huge measles outbreak in the United States. This is a highly contagious virus. It can hang out in the air for up to two hours. Today, we have a vaccine for it, but in the 40s, every kid in this room was vulnerable, so to protect them, researchers ran an experiment with this device that emitted ultraviolet light, and something crazy happened. In rooms with no UV chambers, over half the children got measles. But in these rooms, the number of kids who got sick was less than 15%. What? But wait, isn't UV light dangerous? Doesn't it cause cancer? Well, only sometimes. So UV light that comes from the sun has a wide range of wavelengths that penetrate deep into the skin and damage DNA. So researchers searched the UV spectrum until they found a type of light that's safe for humans, and they landed here on something called far UV. The wavelengths here are too small to penetrate the skin, so they won't cause cancer, but they still wipe out germs. So now we're looking for ways that we can use light as a disinfectant in our everyday lives. So if you believe there should be more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.

What if a big asteroid were headed for Earth? What are the three most realistic ways we could stop it? So in 2013, the United Nations created a special group of space agencies to decide between the options in exactly this situation. Option one, you do nothing. There's always a chance it'll miss you, but maybe not. So option two, you smash a spacecraft into it to change its course just enough that it misses Earth. We've tested this in real life. In 2022, NASA deliberately slammed a spacecraft the size of a vending machine into an asteroid the size of a few city blocks, and it worked. It significantly changed the asteroid's path. 25 times more than they expected, and their blueprints for an even bigger one. And now, option three, you nuke the asteroid. But not to explode it, because that would probably just make a bunch of dangerous fragments that might get pulled back together by gravity and hit us anyway. So instead, the idea is to nuke just one side of it, vaporizing part of its surface, turning the asteroid into a rocket, pushing it in the opposite direction. And there are two more wild options here as well. To see those, watch our longer video with Mark Rober. Or so follow along.

I wish I knew sooner how insanely strong an airplane's wings are. What I'm about to show you cured my fear of flying. So a few months ago, I went on a zero gravity flight, which they do in a normal commercial plane. But to make you feel weightless in that plane, mid flight, the pilots have to go full thrust on the engines and pitch the plane into a sharp 47 degree incline along this path. And during this part, you feel squished to the floor, experiencing 1.8 Gs or hyper gravity. But the plane is also experiencing those forces. It's like putting the weight of a second plane onto the wings, which is surprisingly totally fine. An aircraft's wings are one of the strongest parts of a plane. They're mounted to the wing box, which is the primary low bearing structure of the wing. And attached to it are front and back wing spars that run the entire length and are supported by ribs in between. And this makes the wings not only strong, but flexible. During a normal commercial flight, the tips of this plane's wings might bend as much as two meters. But during the steepest parts of a zero G maneuver, the wing tips can flex as far as three meters. They actually test this on the ground, like this. And they don't break under five meters. And I've thought about that every single time I've been on a little bumpy flight since. So for more optimistic science and textories, follow along.

Look at this. This is the cockpit in a zero gravity plane. Do you notice anything weird? The first pilot is in charge of the plane's left right tilt of the wings, called the roll. Their job is to make sure that the wings stay level. The second pilot sits in the middle and controls the engines, the throttle. They have to create just enough thrust so that the plane doesn't move in reference to you. Too much thrust in the plane goes forward, moving you to the back, and too little thrust then you drift toward the front. And neither of those is perfect zero G. But the hardest part of all is the final pilot's job. Because they control the upward or downward tilt of the plane, the pitch. Remember, they have to follow this arc. But to make things more complicated, the plane's nose can't point in the exact direction it's traveling. If it did, the wings would get lift and zero gravity would go away again. So instead, the plane needs the point slightly under the direction of travel. But that direction isn't a straight line, it's a constantly changing curve. And on top of that, the exact angle varies with air pressure and velocity. This is insanely hard, but they do it 31 times in a single flight. All to do experiments for space. Your Christmas lights almost didn't exist. Let me show you why. So to make white light, you need three colors. Red and green will make yellow. And for all white LED light, you need blue. But this was a big problem because for a long time, we can only make red and green, which make yellow. It took decades longer to make blue because energy. LED light is made when electrons dance. So when you give electrons a push of energy, they jump to an excited state, but they don't want to stay there for long. They fall back down. And that fall releases energy as heat and light. And the height of the fall determines the color emitted. A little drop is less energy, and that makes red a longer wavelength. And a big drop is more energy and makes blue a shorter wavelength. So the challenge was finding a material that could create those higher energy jumps. It took us until the 90s to find that perfect material. Gallium nitrite. And now that we have scientists are getting even better at making super efficient, deep blue LEDs with other materials. If you want more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.
